topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the dorm room had become a heavy, suffocating blanket. I was halfway through sketching in my worn leather-bound book, the scent of graphite sharp in the air, when I glanced over. Liam was sitting at his desk, ostensibly studying, but his eyes kept flicking toward my side of the room. My heart started a frantic drumming against my ribs. I slowly lowered my sketchbook, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to appear casual. He looked up, a flicker of something unreadable in his gaze, and immediately dropped his eyes back to his textbook, a small, almost imperceptible tightening around his jaw betraying him. I quickly slammed the book shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet space. I needed to find an excuse to leave, to escape the suffocating tension building between us.

[story 2]

It happened during the late-night study session. I was trying to focus on differential equations, but every few minutes, I’d feel a prickle on the back of my neck. I risked a peek at Liam’s side of the room. His laptop was open, but it wasn't displaying code; it was a physical book, the pages slightly askew. My breath hitched. I watched, frozen, as his finger traced a line of text, his expression intensely concentrated. A cold wave washed over me, making my hands clammy. I swallowed hard, my mouth suddenly dry. When he finally looked up, catching my stare, his eyes widened just a fraction, and he immediately closed the book with a swift, almost defensive motion. I mumbled something about needing water, my voice sounding reedy and too high, and scrambled to move to the opposite side of the room, acutely aware of every shift in the floorboards beneath my feet.

[story 3]

I found it tucked beneath a pile of textbooks—my journal. My stomach clenched instantly. I hadn't locked my desk drawer properly. When I opened it, my breath caught in my throat. The handwriting inside was mine, detailed entries about anxieties and dreams I hadn't even fully processed yet. Then I saw it: a bookmark, placed precisely on a page describing a recent panic attack. My gaze snapped toward Liam, who was across the room, ostensibly sorting laundry. He was standing still, his shoulders rigid. The air seemed to thicken, pressing down on my chest. I wanted to bolt, to vanish, but my feet felt rooted to the linoleum. I forced myself to look away, focusing intently on a dust bunny near the window, praying that he wouldn't notice the incriminating evidence lying in my open book.
